coredata.dc:description: Here, a smartphone-assisted dual-color ratiometric fluorescence smart gel label-based visual sensing platform was constructed for real-time evaluation of the freshness of agro-food based on the biogenic amines responses. Green-emission fluorescence carbon dots (CDs) coupled with blue-emission fluorescence bimetallic metal–organic framework (Fe/Zr-MOF) obtained dual-color CDs@Fe/Zr-MOF fluorescence nanoprobe acts as the response units. With the increase of SP and HIS content, the green fluorescence of CDs was enhanced, while the blue fluorescence of Fe/Zr-MOF was quenched. Therefore, this dual-color probe achieved a clear fluorescence color response to biogenic amines. The nanoprobe possessed sensitive and color-responsive with the LODs of 0.17 μM for SP and 2.95 μM for HIS in a wide range of 0–937.5 µM, respectively. Besides, these fluorescent nanoprobes were immobilized on the hydrogel carrier, and the intelligent fluorescent hydrogel tag can be obtained after freeze-drying, which realizes the real-time qualitative monitoring of SP and HIS in pork and shrimp samples.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors wish it to be known that, in their opinions, Zhiwei Lu and Mengjiao Li should be regarded as joint First Authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Deep learning-assisted smartphone optical sensing platform based on CDs@Fe-Zr-MOF smart label detects SP and HIS, and meat freshness.

Here, a smartphone-assisted dual-color ratiometric fluorescence smart gel label-based visual sensing platform was constructed for real-time evaluation of the freshness of agro-food based on the biogenic amines responses. Green-emission fluorescence carbon dots (CDs) coupled with blue-emission fluorescence bimetallic metal–organic framework (Fe/Zr-MOF) obtained dual-color CDs@Fe/Zr-MOF fluorescence nanoprobe acts as the response units. With the increase of SP and HIS content, the green fluorescence of CDs was enhanced, while the blue fluorescence of Fe/Zr-MOF was quenched. Therefore, this dual-color probe achieved a clear fluorescence color response to biogenic amines. The nanoprobe possessed sensitive and color-responsive with the LODs of 0.17 μM for SP and 2.95 μM for HIS in a wide range of 0–937.5 µM, respectively. Besides, these fluorescent nanoprobes were immobilized on the hydrogel carrier, and the intelligent fluorescent hydrogel tag can be obtained after freeze-drying, which realizes the real-time qualitative monitoring of SP and HIS in pork and shrimp samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, with the improvement of living standards and the explosive growth of food problems, people have paid more and more attention to the problem of food safety. Among them, agro-food corruption is one of the biggest concerns for consumers and the food industry (Li, Gan, Yang, Fu, & Wang, 2021). Not only will agro-food corruption cause huge economic losses and waste of resources, but also cause serious damage to people's health. Biogenic amines (BAs) are basic nitrogen compounds produced by amino acid decarboxylation in the process of food spoilage due to microbial activities or endogenous tissue metabolism (Liu, Xiao, Shang, Zhuang, & Wang, 2022). BAs, including spermine (SP) and histamine (HIS), are present in a variety of foods, especially aquatic products, and meat products, so they can be considered an important indicator for monitoring agro-food quality, freshness and safety (Chen et al., 2022). Excessive consumption of BAs (especially multiple BAs at the same time) can cause allergic reactions such as headaches, nausea, heart palpitations, changes in blood pressure, respiratory disturbances, and threaten life in severe cases (Guo et al., 2022). Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop timely and effective methods to detect and monitor its freshness (Liu, Jiang, et al., 2022).

Common methods for monitoring spermine, histamine, and BAs include liquid chromatography (Tsai, Chen, Liao, Weng, & Feng, 2021), electrochemical detection (Khan et al., 2018), conductive polymer material detection(J. Wu et al., 2023), SERS (Zhou et al., 2020), and UV colorimetry (Wu et al., 2023), whose pretreatments of the sample are complex and require large and expensive instruments. Optical sensing detection, especially fluorescence detection, has attracted much attention due to its operation detection, rapid response, and good selectivity. Common fluorescent materials include organic fluorescent materials and inorganic fluorescent materials. Inorganic fluorescent materials include carbon dots (CDs), metal clusters (MNCs), light-emitting metal–organic frameworks (L-MOFs), etc.

CDs have been widely used in sensing detection, biological imaging, disease diagnosis, and other research fields because of their excellent characteristics such as high water solubility, excellent photostability, small size, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility. It is an ideal internal reference signal for developing a ratiometric fluorescence sensor (Jalili, Irani-nezhad, Khataee, & Joo, 2021; Mousavi, Zare-Dorabei, & Mosavi, 2021). As a new kind of fluorescent material, metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) are a new type of porous material with wide porosity, large internal surface area, chemical and thermal stability, which is a very beneficial functional material. Besides, L-MOFs have a wide prospect in analysis and sensing on account of their stability of chemistry and high surface area (Ding, Liu, & Gref, 2022). Recent studies have confirmed that introducing guest nanomaterials of various shapes and functions into MOF is an important way to synthesize and improve the performance characteristics of composite materials.

Currently, a variety of fluorescence sensors have been used to detect BAs. However, the traditional fluorescence detection method is a single-emission fluorescent probe based on a luminescent material to detect the content of BAs through fluorescence intensity changes (Bao, Shao, Zhou, & Han, 2021). The detection accuracy is possibly affected by the state of fluorescent materials, instrument state, temperature, and humidity. So it is not very advantageous in the detection of BAs. It is worth noting that dual emission ratio fluorescent probes based on two luminescent materials have shown a good application prospect as optical sensors. There are two types of dual emission ratio probes used to detect BAs: Type I is based on the constant intensity of a fluorescent material as an internal reference, and the other fluorescent material is a response signal of Bas (Yan et al., 2022), thus realizing the detection of BAs. Jia et al. published a study in Nature Communications using ratio probes, red fluorescence as a reference, and green fluorescence in response to BAs (Jia et al., 2019). In the other category (Type II), the fluorescence intensity of two kinds of fluorescent materials has opposite changes, and their sensitivity and color contrast are stronger than those of Type I. Xiao's team constructed a dual emission ratio fluorescence probe--MR@Eu-MOFs by combining Eu-MOF with methyl red through a coupling agent (Xu, Lian, Hao, Zhang, & Yan, 2017). Unfortunately, most of the reported articles are Type I ratio probes. While the type II ratio probes having been reported always has a very small change in fluorescence intensity, which is no different from Type I ratio probes in sensitivity. So it is very important to develop and research a type II ratio probe, which has a large change in intensity and high sensitivity.

The main advantage of a fluorescent probe is that it can make some judgments through color changes. However, visual monitoring largely depends on our naked eyes, naked eyes cannot capture small color changes, and different detection personnel has a different sensitivity to color. As a popular detection method, smartphone-assisted fluorescence analysis has been used for the rapid detection of harmful and toxic substances. Compared with visual analysis, chromaticity analysis based on smart-phone can achieve higher sensitivity and accuracy and reduce the error of the naked eye. At the same time, the rapid development of Artificial Intelligence (AI) provides a strong guarantee for the further development of intelligent detection (Ren & Cao, 2019). In particular, Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) and deep learning (DL) can be said to be the most widely used technologies in intelligent monitoring (Zhao et al., 2021). With its strong learning ability, the existing state of objects in the environment can be timely monitored, accelerating the efficient combination of intelligent detection of portable devices.

Currently, the reported analysis methods based on smartphones mainly shoot images and capture fluorescence signals through the camera system, and extract red-green-blue (RGB), hue-saturation-lightness (HSV) values by using the ordinary cutting algorithm and YOLO V3 deep learning algorithm to achieve the purpose of turning optical signals into digital signals. The linear relationship between the concentration and RGB or HSV is obtained, and then an intelligent analysis and sensing system is established. Our research group has successfully constructed smart detection platforms based on smartphones (Lu, Li, et al., 2022). For example, Lu et al. constructed smart detection of heavy metal ions and pesticides with ratio fluorescence materials for the detection of fruits and vegetables (Lu, Chen, et al., 2022; Wang, Jin et al., 2022; Wang, Zhu et al., 2022). Rao et al. reported a dual-emission probe based on the smartphone for the detection of metal ions and peroxides (Rao et al., 2020). However, the detection of BAs by two-color probes with high sensitivity based on smartphone detection systems has rarely been reported.

Rather than testing after eating, consumers want to know whether food is fresh or not before consuming it. However, these reported fluorescence detection methods cannot be on-site detection. Based on the above discussion, fluorescent labels (with built-in sensors in food bags) should be considered. When the food is rotten, it cannot destroy the food packaging, inform consumers of the freshness of food and protect the rights and interests of consumers. If the food is not rotten, it can be sold normally afterward, which will not cause a waste of resources, because the meat was not processed during the inspection. In recent years, fluorescent labels were mostly made of fluorescent materials loaded onto the acetate fiber membrane (He, Zhan, Liu, Ruan, & Wu, 2020), and filter paper. (Lu et al., 2021). However, the loaded fluorescent materials mainly adopt the method of soaking, so it is impossible to add fluorescence to the production. Therefore, it is difficult to ensure uniform and disperse fluorescent materials. Various nanomaterials have been effectively combined with hydrogels to form new hybrid hydrogels, showing different functions (Kailasa, Joshi, Kateshiya, Koduru, & Malek, 2022). Hydrogels have gradually become one of the important carriers of fluorescent materials due to their good luminescence properties and porous structure (Wei et al., 2021). The fluorescent material is easily loaded evenly onto the hydrogel to maintain good luminescence properties.

Among BAs, SP is the biogenic amine that is produced more in the process of meat deterioration, and HIS is the biogenic amine that is more harmful to human health (Iko Afé et al., 2021). Therefore, we propose a detection platform assisted by deep learning and a smartphone to detect SP and HIS. Based on this principle, a fluorescent smart label was created to detect the freshness of shrimp and pork. Firstly, the blue fluorescent iron zirconium organic frame (Fe/Zr-MOF) (Scheme 1
b) and the green fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) (Scheme 1 a) were synthesized. The two materials were combined by electrostatic interaction to form a two-color fluorescent probe denoted as CDs@Fe/Zr-MOF (Scheme 1 c). The synthesized CDs@Fe/Zr-MOF composite not only has the advantages of a ratiometric fluorescence sensor but also can accumulate target analytes strongly due to the adsorption capacity of Fe/Zr-MOFs. After adding SP or HIS, the color of the system changed from blue to green. Besides, after loading the two-color fluorescent probe on the hydrogel (Scheme 1 d), the intelligent fluorescent hydrogel label obtained can be used for qualitative monitoring of freshness. The smart fluorescent label is a new possibility for freshness monitoring and has a certain market prospect.

O-dihydroxybenzene, ethanediamin, Zr (NO3)4·5H2O, Fe (NO3)3·9H2O, 2-amino terephthalic acid, N, N-dimethylformamide, ethylic acid (DMF), spermine (SP), histamine (HIS), HCl, NaOH, absolute ethyl alcohol, phosphate buffer, KCl, ZnCl2, MgCl2, NaCl, CaCl2, ZnCl2 glycine (Gly), alanine (Ala), threonine (Thr), methionine (Met), phenylalanine (Phe), glutamic acid (Glu), proline (Pro), tryptophan (Try). Buffers of different pH were prepared with 20 mM NaH2PO4-Na2HPO4 solution. Ultrapure water (18.23 MΩ cm). The pork and shrimp were purchased from a local supermarket.

Fluorescence spectra were displayed on a fluorescence spectrometer (Parkinmer Instruments ltd, Rand, UK). The absorption spectra of the materials were tested on a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Agilent Cary60, USA). Zeta potential data were measured using a Malvern Zetaser machine (Zetaser WT, UK). Fluorescence lifetimes were recorded using a time-resolved fluorescence spectrometer (PLS 980, Edinburgh, UK). The quantum yield (PLQY) of the material was measured on a spectrophotometer (Horibafluoromax-4, FR) equipped with a Quanta-φF-3029 integrating sphere. Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR-8400S, Shimadzu, Japan) was used to detect the surface functional groups of the materials. The morphology of Fe/Zr-MOF and CDs was determined by scanning transmission electron microscopy (TEM; JEOL JEM2100F) and electron microscopy (SEM; Zeiss Supra55). The elemental composition and valence of the material were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Shimadzu, Japan). The crystalline phases of Fe/Zr-MOF and CDs were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, DX-2700, Dandong, China). The photos in the article were all taken with a Huawei P30 smartphone.

Synthesize CDs from previous reports with minor modifications (Han et al., 2017). 0.2202 g catechol was weighed and put into a 20 mL water beaker, stirred, and dissolved, and 67 μL ethylenediamine solution was added to it. After stirring and mixing, the material was heated and stirred in a water bath at 50 ℃ for 2 h. The material was filtered through a filter membrane, and the solution was placed in a refrigerator at 4 °C for refrigeration.

Fe/Zr-MOF was synthesized according to the classical synthesis method previously reported with some modifications (Li et al., 2020). 1.33 mmol Fe (NO3)3·9H2O, 1.33 mmol Zr (NO3)4·5H2O, and 1.33 mmol 2-amino-terephthalic acid were dissolved in 5 mL, 5 mL, and 10 mL DMF, respectively. Next, the prepared Zr (NO3)4 solution, Fe (NO3)3 solution, and 2-amino-terephthalic acid solution, were thoroughly mixed with 5 mL DMF and 5 mL CH3COOH, and then transferred to a 50 mL hydrothermal reactor for reaction at 120 °C for 12 h. After reacting, the product was removed and washed several times with acetone. After drying overnight at 40 °C, 10 mg of the material was dissolved into a 1:1 mixture of 50 mL acetic acid and aqueous solution, and the solution was refrigerated at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of SP and HIS
ce:section.ce:para: A certain amount of CDs and Fe/Zr-MOF were added into the phosphate buffer solution prepared in advance (pH = 5.0) and mixed thoroughly. A two-color fluorescent probe solution of 1980 μL was added into a 2 mL PC tube, and then SP and HIS solutions of different concentrations were added to the solution. After 30 s reaction at room temperature, the fluorescence emission spectra of the mixture of the two materials were measured by UV irradiation. The slit widths for excitation and emission were set to 20 nm and 15 nm.

Modified according to the previous literature (Wang, Jin et al., 2022; Wang, Zhu et al., 2022). Add 3.8 g sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) to 100 mL boiling water. Stir and heat continuously until CMC completely dissolves and becomes a viscous liquid, and stop heating. After the thick liquid was cooled to 40 °C, add 1 mL of CDs solution and 3 mL of Fe/Zr-MOF solution. Stir evenly and pour into the Petri dish. Put the material in the lower layer of the refrigerator at −18 °C for two days, then take it out to the vacuum freeze dryer, and dry it for about 2 days. Take out the hydrogel label completely and cut it into the same size and shape when using.

To evaluate the universality of the two-color fluorescent label, fresh pork and shrimp meat purchased from supermarkets were selected as real samples for freshness detection. Firstly, use a clean knife to cut the same parts of pork into samples of the same quality and similar shape, and select the whole shrimp of basically the same size. After grouping, the qualitative determination was carried out through an intelligent fluorescent hydrogel label. The experiment was divided into three temperatures, which were −20 °C, 4 °C, and 35 °C respectively. Different meats were placed at these three temperatures for 0–48 h, during which the fluorescent labels were photographed and recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The establishment of smartphone devices based on deep learning
ce:section.ce:para: The smartphone-based device mainly includes a camera cartridge and a smartphone. Set up a camera environment with a camera obscura. The smartphone is used to take pictures. The camera obscura is a 3D printing plastic imaging module developed in cooperation with Dongguan Huitong Handban Model Co., ltd. based on black resin. The cartridge includes an outer frame, a tube slot, a mobile phone holder, a top cover, and a portable ultraviolet lamp. The outer box is designed as a rectangle (180 mm*150 mm*100 mm). The tube tank (13 mm*13 mm*37 mm) has 11 independent test tube placement positions, which do not interfere with each other. The phone holder fits well into the outer box. The missing part of the outer box can be used to hold the phone. The mobile phone can take pictures through the camera hole (42 mm*14 mm) on the holder. And the smartphone's camera is parallel to the tube slot, which allows the same results to be obtained under the same conditions. Portable UV lamps with external power supply have two kinds of emission light (λex = 365 nm and λex = 254 nm). The smartphone model is Huawei P30 Pro. Fluorescent photos are taken by the smartphone's HD rear camera (specs: 40 megapixels; 20 million pixels; 8 megapixels) shot. Then, WeChat mini program is used to convert the color of fluorescent photos into RGB values, and a linear relationship between detection substance concentration and RGB is established.

Firstly, TEM and SEM were used to measure the size characterization and morphology. CDs are uniformly dispersed and similar to spherical points in morphology ( Fig. 1
a). The diameter is mainly distributed between 1.0 and 5.0 nm ( Fig. 1 c), and the average diameter is 2.4792 nm. Fe/Zr-MOF is spindle-shaped ( Fig. 1 d), and the diameter of transverse particles ranged from 349.74 nm to 584.24 nm ( Fig. 1 f), with an average diameter of 457.4902 nm. The HRTEM magnified CDs of the figure show a lattice spacing of 0.216 nm（Fig. 1b), which corresponds to the face (101). The measured lattice spacing of MOF is 0.202 nm ( Fig. 1 f), which corresponds to the surface (200). In addition, the distribution of elements C, Fe, N, and Zr is uniform in the EDS mapping figure ( Fig. 1 h), which proves the successful bimetallic doping. Fig. S1 f showed the XRD pattern of the prepared MOF, which is similar to the Fe/Zr-MOF synthesized in the literature. The product exhibited significant characteristic diffraction peaks at 7.3°, 8.4°, and 25.8°, corresponding to the (110), (200), and (600) planes, respectively. This result indicated that bimetallic Fe/Zr-MOF had been successfully prepared.

The surface groups of CDs and Fe/Zr-MOF can be studied by infrared spectroscopy ( Fig. S1 c, d). The characteristic absorption bands of CDs at 3347.56 cm−1 and 3128.94 cm−1 are the stretching vibration of OH and NH, respectively. The peak value of 1756.16 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibration of CO on the benzene ring, and the intensity of 741.08 cm−1 is owing to the vibration of the adjacent displacement on the benzene ring. The characteristic absorption peak of 1631.32 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of CN. According to the literature, the luminous group is CN, proving the successful synthesis of CDs. Fe/Zr-MOF material showed some typical FTIR absorption peaks. The characteristic peaks of 3455 cm−1 and 3339.01 cm−1 in Fe/Zr-MOF are derived from the stretching vibration of OH and the stretching vibration of NH. The peak around 1659.73 cm−1 should be the vibration of CO. The characteristic peaks at 767.77 cm−1, 661.62 cm−1, and 481.22 cm−1 are derived from Zr-O-Zr vibration, while the peaks at 584.18 cm−1 should be derived from the vibration of the Fe-O-Fe bond. The peak of the infrared spectrum fully proved that the preparation of Fe/Zr-MOF was successful. In addition, XPS results ( Fig. S1 a) also showed that CDs contained elements C, O, and N, accounting for 79.67 %, 13.82 %, and 6.5 %, respectively. The C1s spectrum was deconvolved into three peaks which correspond to CC/CC (284.8 eV), CN/CO (286.5 eV), and CN/CO (288.8 eV), respectively ( Fig. S2 a). The high-resolution O1s spectrum ( Fig. S2 b) had two peaks corresponding to COC/COH (531.8 eV) and CO (533.5 eV), respectively. The high-resolution N1s spectrum ( Fig. S2 c) had two peaks at 399.1 eV and 401.1 eV corresponding to CN and CNC, respectively. These results were consistent with those measured by infrared spectroscopy. The XPS results of Fe/Zr-MOF confirmed that the Fe/Zr-MOF contained C, O, N, Zr, and trace Fe ( Fig. S1 b). The C1s spectrum ( Fig. S3 a) showed three bands at 284.8 eV, 286.5 eV, and 288.8 eV, representing CC/CC, CN/CO, and CN/CO bonds, respectively. The N1s spectrum ( Fig. S3 b) had two peaks at 399.5 eV and 401.7 eV, which were correlated with CN and CNC. XPS spectra of high-resolution O1s ( Fig. S3 c) were decomposed into three peaks at 531.8 eV, 533.5 eV, and 534.3 eV, corresponding to COC/COH, CO, and COOH bonds, respectively. The inset in Fig. S3
d showed the high-resolution XPS of Zr3d, which has been deconvolved into two peaks. These signals belong to Zr3d3/2 (185.0 eV) and Zr3d5/2 (182.7 eV). The Fe2p spectrum ( Fig. S3 e) was divided into four peaks, representing Fe3+2p1/2, Fe3+2p3/2, Fe2+2p1/2, and Fe2+2p3/2, respectively. In addition, in Fig. S4 a, the Zeta potential of CDs is −4.88 mV, indicating that the surface is negatively charged, while the Zeta potential of Fe/Zr-MOF is 7.15 mV ( Fig. S4 d), indicating that the surface is positively charged. The potentials of the two materials are opposite, so electrostatic attraction existed when the two materials were physically mixed. This electrostatic action can combine the two materials well to form a two-color fluorescent probe CDs@Fe/Zr-MOF. Infrared peaks before and after the reaction were also studied. When SP and HIS are added to CDs, the main peak was still retained, which was consistent with CDs. The case of Fe/Zr-MOF and mixed materials was similar to that of CDs. The UV absorption spectra of CDs and Fe/Zr-MOF (
Fig. S6
f) were measured at 405 nm and 400 nm, respectively. However, the UV absorption spectrum of the mixed material had a peak at 450 nm, indicating that the two materials may react.

To better determine SP and HIS by CDs@Fe/Zr-MOF, improve the sensitivity of the two-color fluorescent probe, and reduce the influence of interfering substances, pH ( Fig. S7
a) and reaction time ( Fig. S7
b-c) were optimized. As shown in Fig. S7
a, when the same amount of SP and HIS were added to the CDs@Fe/Zr-MOF two-color fluorescence probe system, it could be seen that the absolute value of fluorescence change of SP with pH = 5.0 was the largest, and pH = 4.0, 5.0, and 6.0 were the pH groups with the maximum absolute value of histamine fluorescence change. Since SP and HIS were measured in the same system, pH = 5 is the optimal condition for the pH test. Then, the reaction time of the fluorescent probe system was studied. As shown in Fig. S7
b-c, when a certain amount of SP and HIS was added to the system, the fluorescence intensity reached the maximum in the 30 s and remained constant thereafter. Therefore, the 30 s is the best response time. Since amines are volatile and samples were tested at various temperatures, the effect of temperature on the system is not studied. Therefore, the optimal conditions for the determination of SP and HIS by two-color fluorescent probe CDs@Fe/Zr-MOF are as follows: pH = 5.0 and the reaction time is 30 s.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of SP and HIS by CDs@Fe/Zr-MOF fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para:
To determine the sensing capability of the proposed two-color fluorescence analysis system, fluorescence emission spectra of SP and HIS systems with different concentrations were measured under optimal experimental conditions. From the results ( Fig. 2
a and 2d), as the SP and HIS increase, the intensity of fluorescence at 450 nm was gradually quenched, while the peak at 505 nm was enhanced. Fig. 2 b shows the relationship between the concentration of SP and the ratio of fluorescence intensity (F508/F450). In the range of SP concentration 0–937.5 μM, F508/F450 has a good linear relationship with SP concentration. The linear equation is y = 0.00203 × + 0.53543 (R2 = 0.99500), the detection limit (LOD) is 0.17 μM (LOD = 3S/K, S represents the standard deviation of three blank samples and K is the slope of the linear relationship). The LOD is comparable to or even lower than most previously reported spermine analysis methods (
Table S1
), and the linear range is equal to or even greater than previously reported analysis methods. The fluorescence emission spectra obtained were converted to CIE chromaticity coordinates ( Fig. 2 c). As the concentration of SP was increased from 0 to 937.5 μM, a significant color change was observed in the chromaticity map. The two-color fluorescent probe changed from blue to green, which was consistent with the observed color change in the cuvette.

In the range of histamine concentration from 0 to 937.5 μM, F508/F450 has a good linear relationship with HIS concentration ( Fig. 2 e). The linear equation is y = 0.0009649 × + 0.6021 (R2 = 0.9916), and the LOD is 2.75 μM. Its detection range is greater than that of many previously reported histamine assays, and its LOD is comparable to or even lower than that of most previously reported HIS assays (
Table S2
). The linear fluorescence emission spectra were converted to CIE chromaticity coordinates ( Fig. 2 f), as expected. As the HIS was increased from 0 to 937.5 μM, a significant color change was observed in the CIE chromaticity map, with the two-color fluorescent probe blue turning to green, essentially the same color as the solution with the corresponding concentration of histamine added. Besides, the fluorescence curves of single CDs and single Fe/Zr-MOF were linearly related to SP and HIS (
Fig. S14-
15). The linear range of CDs alone ( Table S5 ) and Fe/Zr-MOF alone ( Table S6 ) is not as wide as the linear range of CDs@Fe/Zr-MOF and LOD is also higher than CDs@Fe/Zr-MOF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of CDs@Fe/Zr-MOF two-color fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate the selectivity of the CDs@Fe/Zr-MOF two-color fluorescent probe, under the same conditions, a certain concentration of SP and HIS solutions were added to the two-color fluorescence system, which was compared with the system with 10 times the concentration of interfering substances. These include amino acids and metals found in meat that are glycine (Gly), alanine (Ala), threonine (Thr), methionine (Met), phenylalanine (Phe), glutamic acid (Glu), proline (Pro), tryptophan (Try), K+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+ (all chloride ions). After preparation, the fluorescence intensity was tested and analyzed. The results showed the fluorescence ratio of SP and HIS F505/F450 changed greatly, but the fluorescence intensity ratio of other substances did not change, which was no different from the fluorescence intensity ratio of adding water (
Fig. S10
a). The results show that the two-color fluorescent probe has certain selectivity and specificity for spermine and histamine and has a certain anti-interference ability and has a certain ability to detect SP and HIS. To observe the selectivity of CDs@Fe/Zr-MOF to HIS or SP in the presence of SP or HIS, four experiments were conducted. The first group of experiments: in the test tube containing CDs@Fe/Zr-MOF, the interferences were added separately and test the fluorescence intensity. In the test tubes of the first experiment, SP, HIS, and the mixture of SP and HIS were added respectively to test the fluorescence intensity. The results showed that the F505/F450 fluorescence intensity ratio of the fourth mixture with SP and HIS was higher than that with SP or HIS alone in the presence of the interference (
Fig. S10
b), which showed that the effects of SP and HIS are superimposed on each other and do not interfere with each other. It is further indicated that the probe is selective to SP and HIS respectively in the presence of other interferers and the presence of SP and HIS. Thus, this probe can be used in complex actual sample analysis and has broad application prospects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism of SP and HIS by two-color fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: To better understand and research the working mechanism of SP and HIS, the luminescence mechanism of CDs and the binding mechanism of Fe/Zr-MOF with CDs were investigated in detail. The possible luminescence principle of CDs was as follows: Catechol exposed to an alkaline solution in the air was oxidized to form o-quinone. As a nucleophile, ethylenediamine reacted with the CO o-quinone to form a Schiff base containing imine group CN(Han et al., 2017). The infrared peak of 1631.32 cm−1 also confirms the existence of CN. The fluorescence excitation spectra had some overlap with the UV absorption peaks caused by the n-π* transition of CN ( Fig. S5 a), which indicated that CN should be the main cause of the luminescence. Because of the possible incomplete bonds in Fe/Zr-MOF, the coordination number of Fe3+ may be less than 6 and that of Zr4+ may be less than 12(Zheng, Lian, Qin, & Yan, 2018). Bare metal sites may exist in Fe/Zr-MOF. After mixing Fe/Zr-MOF and CDs, the exposed metal sites Fe3+ and Zr4+ complexed with the CN of the carbon dots to form a complex, resulting in the inhibition of CDs fluorescence. After mixing, the UV peak at 405 nm was reduced. A new peak of 450 nm was created. Therefore, the UV absorption of the system was studied ( Fig. S5 c). In a fixed 2 mL system, 1 mL of Fe/Zr-MOF solution was added, and the UV absorption was measured according to the volume ratio of added water and CDs. The UV spectrum of Fe/Zr-MOF is the black line. The red-line is a fixed 2 mL volume with 1 mL of Fe/Zr-MOF and the remaining 1 mL of solution containing CDs to water with a 3:7 vol ratio of UV absorption. The blue line is the UV absorption with a 6:4 vol ratio of CDs to water. The green line is the UV absorption with a 1:0 vol ratio of CDs to water. The purple line is the UV absorption of CDs ( Fig. S5 c) When CDs and water were added to the volume ratio of 3:7, only the peak at 450 nm was found in the UV spectrum. When the volume ratio of CDs and water was 6:4, a weak UV peak appeared at 405 nm. Only a 450 nm UV peak could prove that the mixture might have reacted completely at a volume ratio of 3:7. Depending on where it appears, 405 nm can prove to be the peak of the CDs itself. When the volume ratio of CDs and water is 1:0, the peak at 400 nm increased again, which further indicated that the peak at 450 nm was generated by the reaction between MOF and CDs. Fe3+ and Zr4+ are a kind of coordination centers with a small radius and high charge which form coordination bonds through electron pairs provided by ligands to form coordination compounds. Compared with the Schiff base electron-withdrawing group CN, the electron-donating groups NH2 on SP and HIS are more likely to cooperate with the central ion. There is a fluorescent probe that uses this principle to detect harmful materials. For example, Gao et al.(Gao et al., 2018) let magnesium coordinate with the Schiff base to form a fluorescent complex. After the addition of Tris, the amino group on Tris replaced the Schiff base and complexed with Mg2+, resulting in fluorescence change. The amine could replace the Schiff base above the complex to form a complex with Fe3+ and Zr4+, releasing the CN group. With the increase of SP and HIS concentration, the amount of CN was increased. The fluorescence peak of CDs had a little red shift, which was consistent with the literature report that the content of CN was increased with the red shift of the fluorescence peak. In this process, the suppressed fluorescence was restored, which wAs manifested as enhanced fluorescence. The UV bond at 450 nm was reduced, and the UV bond at 405 nm was recovered ( Fig. S5 d). At the same time, the Zeta potentials of CDs and Fe/Zr-MOF were measured, and there was electrostatic interaction between CDs and Fe/Zr-MOF, which further confirmed the combination of CDs and Fe/Zr-MOF.

Fe/Zr-MOF had a large surface area (Wu et al., 2021), which made it easier for SP and HIS to contact Fe/Zr-MOF. When SP and HIS solutions were added, CDs complexation with Fe/Zr-MOF decreased. The Zeta potentials of the Fe/Zr-MOF system were measured before and after adding the assay ( Fig. S4 d-f and
Fig. S17
a). The surface of Fe/Zr-MOF was positively charged and its Zeta potential was + 7.15 mV. The Zeta potentials of SP and HIS solutions added to Fe/Zr-MOF were + 4.99 mV and + 1.02 mV. The above information showed the absolute value of the potential of Fe/Zr-MOF decreased after the addition of the two kinds of detectors. It was proved that SP and HIS have electrostatic interaction with MOF. In addition, the fluorescence lifetimes were measured (
Fig. S8
d-f). From 17.54 ns to 15.72 ns and 15.48 ns, the fluorescence lifetimes changed little, which may be static quenching. By observing the UV absorption peak and fluorescence emission peak ( Fig. S5 b), it could be seen that Fe/Zr-MOF has a UV peak at 400 nm and a fluorescence emission peak at 450 nm. Through static quenching, the partial overlap between the fluorescence peak and UV peak, and Zeta potential, it can be seen that the internal filtration effect and electrostatic interaction of Fe/Zr-MOF caused the reduction of the fluorescence intensity.

More interestingly, the fluorescence of CDs alone was quenched by the addition of SP solution and HIS solution (
Fig. S13
b-c). At the same time, the fluorescence lifetime was less. As shown in Fig. S8 , when SP and HIS solutions were added to CDs, the fluorescence lifetime decreased from 4.88 ns to 3.69 ns and 3.77 ns. There was only a slight change in the intensity of the UV peak before and after adding the detection substance, indicating that no new substance was produced. The mixed system was measured at PH = 5.0, in which the solvent of Fe/Zr-MOF was partially acetic acid. The mixed system was also difficult to deprotonate after the addition of SP and HIS. There was an ethylenediamine solution in the CDs system, and the solution itself was already alkaline. When SP was added to the CDs system, obvious deprotonation occurred (Wu et al., 2014), which caused a change in surface charge and resulted in a decrease in fluorescence. The Zeta potentials were measured before and after the addition of CDs, and the Zeta potential became smaller, which was consistent with the previously reported changes in Zeta potential and pH (Wu et al., 2014), further proving that the speculation was correct. Thus, this experiment proposed a new possibility of constructing ratio probes through the interaction of two probes and then achieved the purpose of improving the detection sensitivity. This method broadens the idea of fluorescence ratio probe design and has certain application significance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Deep learning-assisted smartphone-assisted sensing platform for SP and HIS detection
ce:section.ce:para:
The specific formula is as follows (Chen, Shi, & Deng, 2021):
(1)
precision =
TP
TP + FP
(2)
Recall =
TP
TP + FN
(3)
F 1 =
2 ∗ Pr ec i s i o n ∗ R ecall
P recision + R ecall
(4)
FDR = 1 - P ercision =
FP
TP + F P
(5)
FNR = 1 - R ecall =
FN
TP + F N

The real and valid object of the image that can be identified by the algorithm is called TP (true positive). The non-real object of the image detected by the algorithm is called FP (false positive). The object that should be detected by the algorithm is called FN (false negative), but the object that is not detected. Accuracy represents the number of correct tests administered. The Recall represents the value of the real valid tube/total tube detected by the algorithm. The F1 score is used as a comprehensive evaluation between recall and accuracy to express the comprehensive performance of the model. According to the above formula, the accuracy is 92.5 %. F1 is 96.1 %. The recall rate was 100.0 %. The FPR was 7.5 %, while the FNR was 0. The recall rate of 100 % and FNR of 0 % indicate that all valid objects in this platform are successfully detected by the algorithm, while the FPR is 7.5 %. Although there is a certain misjudgment rate, this parameter is still relatively good. To sum up, it is proved that the platform has good performance, is very effective, and can be put into use.
The detailed operation flow is as follows:

First, adjust the smartphone to the best parameter professional mode to take pictures. Appropriate photos are uploaded to the applet. In the label interface, name the image, and label the number of cuttings. Then according to the different concentrations of SP or HIS, set the corresponding concentration. The small program uses the YOLO V3 algorithm to automatically classify the uploaded images and identify cuvettes. The extraction is carried out according to the color and shade of the cuttings. The algorithm identifies the RGB and HSV values for each cuvette. Depending on the extracted cuvette, it corresponds to the filled concentration. Display the RGB of each test tube in the applet interface. Then select the data processing channel, which includes H, S, V, R/B, G/B, G, S/V, R, B, and (R + G)/B values. As the increase of SP and HIS concentrations, the color of cuttings changed from blue to green, which was a process of blue weakening and green enhancement, so G/B was used to fit the data. The linear relationship between the G/B value and SP or HIS is obtained. The linear equation and R2 can be displayed directly on the smartphone, and there is no need to import the data into the computer for fitting.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone fluorescence sensing platform to detect SP and HIS
ce:section.ce:para:
Relying on the proposed applet above, a smartphone fluorescence sensing platform is put into use, which combines two-color fluorescence probes with deep learning-assisted smartphones. Firstly, the system image was obtained by taking a photo with the smartphone, and then the image was uploaded to the WeChat applet. The YOLO V3 algorithm was used to classify, extract the system images and convert them into RGB values. As the concentration of SP and HIS increased, the blue-fluorescence of the dual-color fluorescent probe was quenched, which meant that the B value decreased. At the same time, the enhanced green fluorescence indicated that the G value increased. Therefore, there was a linear relationship between the G/B value and the concentration of SP and HIS. As shown in Fig. 4
, the equation between the G/B ratio and SP concentration in the concentration range of 0–1000 μM is y = 0.0005 x + 0.4920 (R2 = 0.9953). From Fig. 4 , it can be seen that there is a good linear correlation between G/B and HIS with the equation y = 0.0003 x + 0.6058 (R2 = 0.9915) in the concentration range of 0–1000 μM. Compared with other chroma channels, G/B has the best linear relationship with SP (
Fig. S11
and
Table S3
) and HIS (
Fig. S12
and
Table S4
). The above results indicate that the portable smartphone-assisted fluorescence sensing platform has much application potential in the real-time detection of SP and HIS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smart fluorescent sensing labels for real sample detection
ce:section.ce:para:
To test the stability of the hydrogel labels and exclude the influence of the hydrogel labels themselves, the fluorescent labels were stored for 0–6 days at the three temperatures (−20 °C, 4 °C, and 35 °C) tested in real samples. The results were shown in Fig. S16 . Under the three temperatures, the color and shape of hydrogel labels remained unchanged no matter whether under sunlight (
Fig. S16
b) or ultraviolet lamp (
Fig. S16
a), indicating that fluorescent hydrogel labels have a certain stability and can be used to detect freshness. Therefore, the results show that the fluorescence intensity and basic morphology of hydrogel tags do not change with time (over 6 consecutive days) at different temperatures (−20 to 35 °C), indicating that the smart tags used in this system have good stability.

Under the condition of 35 °C storage, the fluorescent label of pork changed from blue to blue-green and dark green with the increase in storage time. The 5 h fluorescent label appeared blue with a hint of green, indicating that the pork had been slightly rotted. The 24 h fluorescent label was green, indicating that the pork had completely rotted. And as time went on, the pork got darker and darker in the sunlight. Under the condition of 4 °C the fluorescence label presented blue with a hint of green after 48 h. The color under the fluorescent lamp did not change much. The color of the fluorescent label at −20 °C was almost unchanged, which not only indicated that the label had a certain detection ability but also indicated the optical stability of the fluorescent label under freezing conditions. There was also no significant change in the shape of the pork label.

As can be seen from the image under the UV lamp of the shrimp, the fluorescent label of the shrimp changed to blue-green after the shrimp was sealed at 35 °C for 5 h, which was more obvious than the green color of the fluorescent label in the pork at the same period, indicating that the shrimp was more perishable than the pork. The color of the shrimp in the sunlight didn't change much. After 24 h of storage, the fluorescent label was soaked with liquid produced by shrimp decay, but green fluorescence could still be observed. By this time, the shrimp had turned red in the sunlight. After that, the fluorescent tags became more and more soaked with the liquid produced by the rotting shrimp. The fluorescent label had a faint dark green color. The blue-green appearance of shrimp meat after storage at 4 °C was the 24th hour of storage. Shrimp in the sun seemed normal to the naked eye. At 29 h and 48 h of storage, the shrimp turned significantly black, and the fluorescent label also turned significantly green, indicating that the shrimp may have rotted at the previous time. The above-mentioned storage at 4 °C showed that the label is a step ahead of the eye. The fluorescence label of the shrimp stored at −20 °C did not turn green within 48 h, but remained blue, indicating that the shrimp did not rot under freezing conditions.

The problem of label soggy is explained as follows. The fluorescent label for pork is basically complete. Part of the black color on the label is caused by contact with the pork during handling and placement, which could have been avoided by gentler handling. And the shrimp is wetter, especially the rotten fish and shrimp. That's mainly because the shrimp itself carries a lot of water. More liquid is produced during decay, enough to make the hydrogel tag soggy. Avoid this by placing the label in a smaller petri dish before placing it in the shrimp. But in this case, it also shows a rule, the wetter the fluorescent label, the more rotten the meat. So the degree of immersion can be used as an auxiliary term to evaluate the freshness of the meat.

The above phenomena indicated that both the onset time of meat decay and the degree of label damage indicate that meat is more likely to produce BAs at higher temperatures. Optical stability of fluorescent tags under various conditions. Most importantly, the fluorescent label has a certain detection ability and good analytical performance, which can be used in the qualitative analysis of pork and shrimp meat, providing a possibility for the detection of food quality and safety.

Herein, a deep learning-assisted smartphone-integrated optical platform based on two-color ratiometric fluorescent probes is investigated to detect SP and HIS, thereby enabling qualitative analysis of meat freshness. Using the electrostatic attraction and complexation reaction of green fluorescent CDs and blue fluorescent Fe/Zr-MOF, a robust fluorescence sensing analysis platform for SP and HIS detection was constructed. As the SP and HIS increased, the green fluorescence intensity of CDs was enhanced, while the blue fluorescence of Fe/Zr-MOF was quenched. The linear ranges SP and HIS were 0–937.5 μM. The LOD of SP was 0.17 μM, while the LOD of HIS is 2.95 μM, enabling accurate detection of SP and HIS. Furthermore, the system of fluorescence color was changed from blue to green with increasing SP and HIS concentrations. Therefore, a portable SP and HIS fluorescence detection and sensing platform based on the smartphone can be developed by utilizing the chroma extraction and processing function of the smartphone. According to the change of fluorescence color, a possibility of qualitative analysis of meat freshness is proposed. Smart labels designed and based on CDs@Fe/Zr-MOF can realize the real-time analysis of food freshness. It has the characteristics of simple operation and rapid reaction and has certain market application prospects.
